1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth is the LLM's per-step order quantities scored deterministically via ΔNLL against Sterman's 11 calibrated human sessions and a leave-one-human-out baseline (compute_delta_nll, nll_gaussian). Scoring is fully reproducible numerical logic with warmup handling and N-clamping; no ambiguity.

2_construct_validity.justification: The headline eval genuinely calls the model (llm.prompt) for all 4 roles over 24 rounds and scores its actual orders. The folder ships paired conditions (nb_task-1 baseline prompt vs the enhanced 'decision guidelines' prompt) that manipulate the same mechanics, isolating whether the model can inhibit prepotent over-ordering (the bullwhip effect) versus instruction scaffolding.

3_code_quality.justification: Clean, modular single-cell implementation: separate Player class with eta-based pipelines, a Sterman simulator, ΔNLL functions, robust parse_order_from_response with line-skipping fallback and a 0 default, and kbench @task/assertions. Lacks markdown but code itself is well-structured and commented.

4_sample_size.justification: NUM_SESSIONS=11, each producing 4 role time-series scored independently (44 ΔNLL units per task variant) over 24 rounds; far exceeds 20 evaluation items.

5_metric_design.justification: Only a single performance dimension is reported (mean ΔNLL/step across sessions and roles). It is a sophisticated calibrated metric, but no second distinct dimension (e.g. cost, calibration) is computed, and justification is via docstring only.

6_problem_statement.justification: Only the in-notebook docstring/title ('Baseline Executive Control — Beer Distribution Game') names the capability with minimal motivation; no broader research framing or gap statement is available as a task page.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Code is self-explanatory with section banners, but there are no markdown cells walking through dataset provenance (the embedded Sterman parameters), the ΔNLL scoring rationale, or metric interpretation; reproduction requires reading code.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (inhibiting over-optimization / bullwhip amplification) with the baseline-vs-enhanced-prompt pair acting as an experimental control, and an explicit hypothesis that more human-like (less amplified) ordering scores higher ΔNLL.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items vary by the 11 distinct human sessions and 4 supply-chain roles, and ΔNLL is reported per role and session, giving some breakdown; but there is no explicit difficulty taxonomy or labeled sub-condition tiering.

10_model_coverage.justification: The task is wired to the single kbench.llm abstraction; no model identifiers appear and only one model is run.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Each sub-task has unambiguous deterministic ground truth: vision uses exact integer goal-count match, physics uses exact MCQ letter match with a parser, and go/no-go uses a deterministic pass/fail (GO only after DISTANCE==0) plus reward-threshold checks in the full Unity env.

2_construct_validity.justification: Headline evals genuinely call the model (llm.respond with images/schema in vision/physics, live AnimalAIEnvironment loop in the full task). Construct is tightly isolated via paired control (0-goal / perception) vs test conditions and shuffled MCQ options; the go/no-go directly operationalizes inhibitory control (withhold GO while DISTANCE>0).

3_code_quality.justification: Idiomatic kbench SDK usage: Actor subclasses, dataclasses, @task with evaluate() over datasets, fresh chats.new per trial, deterministic per-trial RNG for option shuffling, and graceful parse fallbacks (default WAIT, non-numeric counted incorrect).

4_sample_size.justification: Dataset ships 297 configs and 297 frame directories; vision test ran 143 trials, physics ~90, and the text go/no-go uses 5 scenarios x10 repeats (50 runs). Well above 20.

5_metric_design.justification: The suite computes distinct dimensions across conditions (perception counting accuracy, physics-prediction accuracy, and reward/inhibition pass-rate), and the physics task deliberately shuffles MCQ option order per trial to mitigate position bias.

6_problem_statement.justification: Markdown clearly names the capability (embodied inhibitory control, go/no-go) and states the rules and pass/fail criteria, but does not situate the task against existing benchmarks or articulate the gap it fills.

7_technical_documentation.justification: Markdown documents the environment, rules, state variable semantics, and pass/fail logic; the config/frame naming schema is parsed and explained in code, and the response schema and scoring are explicit, enabling reproduction.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Probes a specific sub-capability (prepotent-response inhibition) with an explicit hypothesis and control/test conditions; separating perception (vision/physics) from action timing lets failures be attributed to inhibition rather than confounds.

9_item_diversity.justification: Items are organized by a structured taxonomy (ramp count/slope, main/ramp/wall goal slots = absent/goodgoal/goodgoalmulti) across vision, physics, text, and full-env modalities; trials are filtered and labeled by these conditions, enabling per-condition analysis.

10_model_coverage.justification: Run-result params show exactly two distinct models across the notebooks: google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview and google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview.

1_answer_correctness.justification: Ground truth letter-mapping outputs exist in the dataset YAML and are compared exactly (reply.answers[k]==v), but verification is fragile: strict exact-string YAML matching with no normalization, and any unparsable YAML reply zeroes that whole task's answers.

2_construct_validity.justification: The eval does call the model (llm.prompt per task) and scores rule-induction accuracy, broadly measuring the claimed capability; but it conflates the target with strict output-format compliance — it penalizes any intro response and any parse failure, so failures may reflect instruction-following/formatting rather than reasoning/adaptation.

3_code_quality.justification: Functional single cell with dataclasses, but all 130 tasks are sent in one accumulating conversation (llm.prompt in a loop) risking context overflow, error handling is a broad try/except that swallows all exceptions, and there is no markdown.

4_sample_size.justification: Dataset contains 130 tasks totaling 487 test inputs (printed total_number_of_test_inputs = 487), far exceeding 20 items.

5_metric_design.justification: Only a single metric is computed — a penalized fraction-correct (penalties_factor * correct / total) — with no second distinct performance dimension.

6_problem_statement.justification: The benchmark_task_description names the capability (observe/reason/learn/verify/adapt to overriding mappings) and gives basic motivation, but no broader research framing or comparison to existing evaluations.

7_technical_documentation.justification: There are no markdown cells; the dataset provenance, YAML schema, override semantics, and scoring/penalty rules must be reverse-engineered from code, though the intro_prompt partially documents the protocol.

8_capability_targeting.justification: Targets a named domain (in-context rule induction with adaptation/override) and the dataset embeds override structure, but there is no clean experimental control isolating adaptation from base induction, so it doesn't pin a single sub-mechanism.

9_item_diversity.justification: Tasks naturally vary in difficulty (growing alphabet, increasing observation/test counts, override chains across task_ids), but results are not reported broken down by any labeled difficulty tier or sub-condition.

10_model_coverage.justification: Run uses the single kbench.llm abstraction; only one model is evaluated.

1_answer_correctness.justification: The benchmark folder is empty — no notebook or any artifact is present, so no ground truth or verification logic exists.

2_construct_validity.justification: No notebook or task page exists; there is no primary evaluation that sends prompts to a model or scores any model output, the strongest construct-validity failure.

3_code_quality.justification: No code or notebook exists for the task, which the rubric explicitly assigns Level 0.

4_sample_size.justification: No evaluation items exist (empty folder), which is fewer than the 1-9 Level-0 range — i.e. zero items.

5_metric_design.justification: No metrics are computed because no notebook exists.

6_problem_statement.justification: No task page text is available beyond the folder name; a reader cannot understand what is measured or why.

7_technical_documentation.justification: No documentation of dataset, methodology, or scoring exists; the folder contains no files.

8_capability_targeting.justification: With no notebook or page, there is no defined capability, hypothesis, or control; nothing can be attributed to any target capability.

9_item_diversity.justification: No items exist, so there is no taxonomy, sub-condition labeling, or performance gradient.

10_model_coverage.justification: No notebook and no runs exist, so the task has been run on zero models.
